did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep neural networks (DNNs) as surrogate models trained on the ISIC 2018 skin lesion dataset to generate adversarial perturbations for physical camera-based attacks. The study evaluated the vulnerability of three DNN-based mobile applications (DermoApp, SkiniveMD, and Blemish Types) for automated skin cancer detection (specifically melanoma vs. nevus classification) to these adversarial attacks, achieving attack success rates of 50-80%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work investigating the vulnerability of three specific mobile applications (DermoApp, SkiniveMD, and Blemish Types) to physical camera-based adversarial attacks. It details a methodology involving the training of surrogate models on the ISIC 2018 dataset, the creation of physical adversarial stickers, and the execution of black-box attacks to measure attack success rates (50-80%). The study includes a 'Materials and methods' section, 'Results' with quantitative data, and concludes with new findings regarding security vulnerabilities, which characterizes it as a research paper rather than a review.

title: Mobile applications for skin cancer detection are vulnerable to physical camera-based adversarial attacks
brief_summary: This study investigates the vulnerability of three deep learning-based mobile applications for skin cancer detection (DermoApp, SkiniveMD, and Blemish Types) to physical camera-based adversarial attacks under black-box conditions. The researchers trained surrogate models on the ISIC 2018 dataset to generate transferable adversarial patterns, which were then implemented as transparent stickers attached to mobile device cameras. The evaluation focused on the applications' ability to distinguish between melanoma and nevus images using real-world physical attacks. Results demonstrated that these attacks successfully manipulated application predictions with success rates ranging from 50% to 80%, with melanoma images showing consistently higher vulnerability than nevus images. The findings reveal significant security concerns regarding the reliability of deployed medical AI applications in clinical settings.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary malignant class studied; used in ISIC 2018 dataset (759 training images) and evaluation dataset (100 test images). Consistently showed higher vulnerability to adversarial attacks compared to nevus.
Primary benign class studied; used in ISIC 2018 dataset (4,679 training images) and evaluation dataset (100 test images). Showed more resilience to adversarial attacks compared to melanoma.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories (melanoma vs. nevus; or multiple skin conditions depending on the application)
all_task_types.details: The study evaluates three mobile applications performing binary classification (melanoma vs. not melanoma for DermoApp) and multiclass classification (seven categories for Blemish Types, various skin conditions for SkiniveMD). The primary experimental focus is on binary classification tasks distinguishing melanoma (MEL) from melanocytic nevus (NV) using the ISIC 2018 dataset. Attack success rates (ASR) are measured based on the manipulation of these discrete class predictions.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A publicly available skin lesion image dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration, used to train surrogate models and generate adversarial perturbations. It contains RGB images classified into seven categories.

use.training: Used to train surrogate models (MobileNetV2, MobileNetV3-Large, ResNet-18, VGG-16, EfficientNet-B1, ResNet-50, VGG-19, DenseNet-121, DenseNet-201, ViT-Base-16, ViT-Small-16) for generating transferable adversarial perturbations.
use.validation: Used to select the best surrogate model performance during training.
use.testing: Used to evaluate surrogate model accuracy and as the source for the evaluation dataset (100 MEL and 100 NV images sampled from the test set) for physical attack experiments.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the effectiveness of adversarial patterns by measuring the proportion of cases where the model's prediction differed from the true label.
To establish a baseline for misclassification rates by comparing adversarial pattern performance against random dot patterns (averaged across 10 different random patterns).
To represent the variability of the average transfer Attack Success Rate across target models in relation to the number of dots.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It reported Attack Success Rates (ASR) and accuracy metrics, comparing adversarial patterns against random patterns and different model architectures, but did not perform statistical hypothesis testing.

architecture_used.main_model: Multiple surrogate models were used: MobileNetV2, MobileNetV3-Large, ResNet-18, VGG-16, EfficientNet-B1, ResNet-50, VGG-19, DenseNet-121, DenseNet-201, ViT-Base-16, and ViT-Small-16.
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained models were utilized; input images were resized to 224 × 224 pixels to match the original training dimensions of the pre-trained models.

architecture_details.description: A diverse set of architectures ranging from mobile-optimized lightweight models to larger counterparts and transformer architectures.

training_details.learning_rate: Initial learning rate of 5.0 × 10−5 for model training; 0.001 for adversarial pattern generation
training_details.learning_rate_schedule: Cosine learning rate schedule with warmup
training_details.data_augmentation: Random augmentation with two operations at magnitude 14
training_details.normalization: Images normalized to 0–1 using mean and standard deviation of 0.5 across all channels
training_details.hardware: Dual NVIDIA A100 GPUs (40GB each) with 192GB system RAM

preprocessing_techniques: Input images resized to 224 × 224 pixels to match pre-trained model dimensions
Image normalization: pixel intensity scaled to 0–1 using mean and standard deviation of 0.5 across all channels
Data augmentation: random augmentation with two operations at magnitude 14
Monitor calibration: brightness set to 250 cd/m² and color temperature to 6500 K
Fixed mounting system to maintain constant distance (approximately 15 cm) and perpendicular angle between camera lens and display surface

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied with two operations at magnitude 14.

conclusions: Mobile applications for skin cancer detection (DermoApp, SkiniveMD, and Blemish Types) are vulnerable to physical camera-based adversarial attacks under black-box conditions.
Attacking transparent stickers attached to mobile device cameras successfully manipulated application predictions with attack success rates ranging from 50% to 80%.
Melanoma images showed consistently higher vulnerability to adversarial attacks compared to nevus images across all tested applications.
Adversarial patterns generated using surrogate models demonstrated high transferability across different deep neural network architectures.
The study reveals significant security concerns regarding the reliability of deployed medical AI applications in clinical settings, as prediction manipulation could affect diagnostic processes.
The findings underscore the importance of security evaluation before deploying deep learning-based mobile applications in clinical practice.
important_remarks: The study is limited to three specific mobile applications (DermoApp, SkiniveMD, and Blemish Types), which may not represent the broader landscape of deployed skin cancer detection apps.
The physical attack evaluation relied on a surrogate model trained on the ISIC 2018 dataset; the actual internal architectures of the target black-box applications were unknown and unverified.

The evaluation dataset consisted of only 100 melanoma and 100 nevus images sampled from the ISIC 2018 test set, limiting the generalizability of the attack success rates to other lesion types or populations.

Physical attacks were conducted using images displayed on a calibrated monitor (DELL U2718Q) rather than direct patient photography, which may not fully replicate the optical properties and variability of real-world skin imaging.
The study did not perform formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values); results are reported as Attack Success Rates (ASR) with standard deviation.

SkiniveMD showed a low baseline accuracy of 57.5% in the evaluation, suggesting that the application's inherent diagnostic reliability was poor, which complicates the interpretation of adversarial vulnerability.

The study did not assess the clinical utility or diagnostic accuracy of the applications against ground truth pathology, relying instead on ISIC 2018 labels which may not reflect real-world clinical complexity.

Adversarial patterns were implemented as transparent stickers on camera lenses; the study notes that different film materials produced similar results, but the specific optical properties of the film used (PLUS Corp. IT-324 F-C) are a constraint.

The research focuses on binary classification (melanoma vs. nevus) for the primary vulnerability analysis, despite some applications claiming multi-class capabilities, potentially overlooking vulnerabilities in other diagnostic categories.
The study acknowledges that it could not implement defensive measures due to the black-box nature of the applications, limiting the scope to vulnerability demonstration rather than mitigation.
